questions.promptTex: \text{What is the common ratio of } 4, 12, 36, 108, \ldots?
\text{Find } a_5 \text{ for } a_1 = 2, r = 3.
steps: Use the explicit formula: $$a_n = a_1 \cdot r^{n-1}$$

questions.promptTex: \text{Which sequence is geometric?}
questions.explanation: $3, 9, 27, 81$ has constant ratio $r = 3$. The first option is squares; the second is arithmetic; the fourth is Fibonacci.
steps: For each, check whether consecutive ratios are equal.
Option 4 (Fibonacci): ratios approach $\varphi$ but aren't constant.

questions.promptTex: 1 + \dfrac{1}{3} + \dfrac{1}{9} + \dfrac{1}{27} + \cdots = ?
steps: Use the infinite sum formula: $$S_\infty = \dfrac{a_1}{1 - r}$$

questions.promptTex: \text{An infinite geometric series converges if and only if:}
questions.explanation: An infinite geometric series converges if and only if $|r| < 1$. Otherwise the terms don't shrink, and the partial sums grow without bound (or oscillate).
steps: For an infinite geometric series, terms go $a_1, a_1 r, a_1 r^2, \ldots$
If $|r| < 1$, the terms shrink toward 0 fast enough that the sum converges.
If $|r| \geq 1$, the terms don't shrink (they stay constant or grow), so the partial sums diverge.

questions.promptTex: \text{A geometric sequence has } a_1 = 5 \text{ and } r = -2. \\ \text{Find } a_6.
hints: $(-2)^5$ is negative because the exponent is odd.
steps: Compute $(-2)^5 = -32$ (odd exponent, so result is negative).

questions.promptTex: \text{In a geometric sequence, } a_3 = 12 \text{ and } a_6 = 96. \\ \text{Find the common ratio } r.
hints: Going from term 3 to term 6 is 3 multiplications by $r$.
steps: Going from $a_3$ to $a_6$ is $6 - 3 = 3$ multiplications by $r$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the sum of the first 10 terms of} \\ 3, 6, 12, 24, \ldots
steps: Use the geometric sum formula: $$S_n = a_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$$

questions.promptTex: \text{The recursive form for } 5, 10, 20, 40, \ldots \text{ is:}
hints: Recursive: each term in terms of the previous one.
Common ratio is $10/5 = 2$, so each term doubles.
questions.explanation: $r = 2$, $a_1 = 5$. Recursive: $a_n = 2 \cdot a_{n-1}$ with $a_1 = 5$. (Option 3 is the explicit form, also valid but not recursive.)
steps: Recursive form needs **two parts**: a starting value and a rule for the next term.
Common ratio: $r = 10/5 = 2$. So each new term is **double** the previous.

questions.promptTex: \text{A geometric series: } 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + \cdots \\ \text{What does the infinite sum equal?}
\text{Which of these series } \textbf{diverges}?
questions.explanation: $1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + \cdots$ has $r = 2$, so $|r| = 2 \geq 1$ — diverges. The others have $|r| = 1/2, 0.9, 1/3$ respectively, all $< 1$, so they converge.
steps: Find $r$ for each (ratio of consecutive terms).

questions.promptTex: \text{An investment of } \$2000 \text{ earns } 8\% \text{ interest per year, } \\ \text{compounded yearly. What is the balance after } 5 \text{ years} \\ \text{(round to nearest dollar)?}
hints: Each year, balance is multiplied by $1.08$.
steps: Each year multiplies the balance by $r = 1 + 0.08 = 1.08$.
Balances form a geometric sequence with $a_1 = 2000$ (year 0) and $r = 1.08$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A bouncing ball drops from height } 10 \text{ m, returning each time} \\ \text{to } 60\% \text{ of its previous height. What is the } \textbf{total} \text{ distance} \\ \text{the ball travels before coming to rest?}
hints: Initial drop: 10 m. Then each bounce goes up and back down — $2 \cdot$ that height.
Bounce heights: $6, 3.6, 2.16, \ldots$ — geometric with $r = 0.6$. Total distance = $10 + 2 \cdot (6 + 3.6 + \cdots) = 10 + 2 \cdot \dfrac{6}{1 - 0.6}$.
steps: First, the ball drops $10$ m straight down.
After the first bounce, it rises $0.6 \cdot 10 = 6$ m, then falls back $6$ m. After the second bounce: rises $3.6$, falls $3.6$. Etc.
Total distance after the initial drop: $$2 \cdot (6 + 3.6 + 2.16 + \cdots)$$
The bounce heights $6, 3.6, 2.16, \ldots$ are geometric with $a_1 = 6$, $r = 0.6$.

questions.promptTex: \text{The series } 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + \cdots + 2^{n-1} \text{ equals which closed form?}
hints: Geometric with $a_1 = 1$, $r = 2$. Use $S_n = a_1 \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Compute } \displaystyle \sum_{k=1}^{8} 5 \cdot 3^{k-1}.
steps: Identify the geometric form: $a_1 = 5$ (first term, $k = 1$), $r = 3$, $n = 8$ terms.

questions.promptTex: \text{Express } 0.\overline{4} \text{ (i.e., } 0.4444\ldots \text{) as a fraction.} \\ \text{Enter the answer as a decimal.}
steps: Write the repeating decimal as an infinite series: $$0.\overline{4} = 0.4 + 0.04 + 0.004 + 0.0004 + \cdots$$
Recognize as geometric with $a_1 = 0.4$ and $r = 0.1$. $|r| < 1$, so it converges.

questions.promptTex: \text{Express the repeating decimal } 0.\overline{36} \text{ (i.e., } 0.363636\ldots) \\ \text{as a fraction. Give the result as a decimal (round to 4 places).}
\text{For the series } a_1 + a_1 r + a_1 r^2 + \cdots, \text{ which condition} \\ \text{makes the infinite sum } \textbf{undefined / divergent}?
questions.explanation: When $|r| = 1$, terms don't shrink (they're $\pm a_1$ forever). Partial sums either grow ($r = 1$) or oscillate ($r = -1$), so the infinite series diverges.
steps: The convergence condition is $|r| < 1$ — strictly less than 1.
$|r| = 1$ ✗ — terms are $a_1, \pm a_1, a_1, \ldots$ which never shrink. Partial sums diverge.

questions.promptTex: \text{An annuity pays you } \$1 \text{ at the end of each year forever.} \\ \text{The discount rate is } 5\% \text{ per year. What is the present value?}
questions.explanation: PV = $\dfrac{1/1.05}{1 - 1/1.05} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20$. (Hence the rule of thumb: a perpetuity paying \$1/year at rate $r$ is worth \$$1/r$.)
steps: Each year's payment, discounted back to today: payment $t$ years from now is worth $\dfrac{1}{1.05^t}$ today.
Total PV is the infinite geometric series: $$\text{PV} = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{1.05^t} = \dfrac{1}{1.05} + \dfrac{1}{1.05^2} + \cdots$$

questions.promptTex: \text{Sum: } 1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{8} + \dfrac{1}{16} \text{ (just these 5 terms).}
steps: Note: as $n \to \infty$, $S_n \to 2$. The remaining gap is $1/16 = 0.0625$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which of the following equals } \dfrac{2}{3}?
hints: Specifying $\overline{66}$ or $\overline{6}$ describes the same number.
questions.explanation: All three notations describe the same infinitely-repeating decimal $0.6666\ldots = \dfrac{2}{3}$.
steps: All three forms describe the same number, $\dfrac{2}{3}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A bouncing ball is dropped from } 8 \text{ m and rebounds to } 75\% \\ \text{of its previous height each bounce. What total vertical distance} \\ \text{does it travel before coming to rest?}
hints: Initial drop: 8. Each subsequent bounce travels up & back down — $2 \times$ that height.
steps: After bounce $k$, the ball rises $0.75^k \cdot 8$ m and then falls the same distance.
Heights $6, 4.5, 3.375, \ldots$ form a geometric sequence with $a_1 = 6$, $r = 0.75$.
Total distance = initial drop + 2 × (sum of bounce heights): $$8 + 2 \cdot 24 = 56 \text{ m}$$

questions.promptTex: \text{A quantity grows by } 8\% \text{ each year. The annual} \\ \textbf{growth factor} \text{ is:}
hints: Growth factor = 1 + (growth rate as a decimal).
questions.explanation: Growth factor = $1 + 0.08 = 1.08$. Each year multiplies the quantity by $1.08$.
steps: Growth means the quantity gets *bigger* — factor must be greater than 1.
(Check: starting with 100, after 1 year: $100 \cdot 1.08 = 108$. Eight more — correct.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A quantity decays by } 30\% \text{ each step. The } \textbf{decay factor} \text{ is:}
hints: Decay factor = 1 - (decay rate as a decimal). It's how much you KEEP each step.
questions.explanation: Decay factor = $1 - 0.30 = 0.70$. Each step keeps 70% of the previous value.
steps: Decay means the quantity gets *smaller* — factor is between 0 and 1.
**Common mistake:** using $0.30$ as the decay factor. That would mean keeping only 30% (i.e., losing 70%) — much faster decay than intended.

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 200 \text{-mg dose of medication is in the body. The body eliminates} \\ 25\% \text{ of the remaining amount each hour. How many mg remain after} \\ 5 \text{ hours? (Round to 1 decimal.)}
steps: Each hour the body eliminates 25%, so it **keeps** 75%. Decay factor $r = 0.75$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A ball is dropped from } 2.0 \text{ m and bounces to } 1.4 \text{ m on the} \\ \text{first bounce. What is its rebound ratio?}
hints: Rebound ratio = (height after bounce) / (height before bounce).
steps: Definition: rebound ratio = height *after* bounce divided by height *before* bounce.

questions.promptTex: \text{Same ball as above (rebound ratio } 0.7 \text{, dropped from } 2.0 \text{ m).} \\ \text{How high does it bounce on the } \textbf{third} \text{ bounce? (round to 3 decimals)}
hints: Each bounce multiplies the height by $r = 0.7$.
steps: Bounce heights form a geometric sequence with $h_0 = 2.0$ and ratio $r = 0.7$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Insert a positive number } x \text{ between } 4 \text{ and } 25 \text{ so that } 4, x, 25 \text{ is geometric.}
hints: In a GP, middle term squared equals product of outers: $x^2 = 4 \cdot 25$.
questions.explanation: Geometric mean property: $x^2 = 4 \cdot 25 = 100$, so $x = 10$. (Common ratio: $r = 10/4 = 5/2$, and indeed $10 \cdot 5/2 = 25$ ✓.)
steps: For three terms in GP: $\dfrac{x}{4} = \dfrac{25}{x}$, i.e., $x^2 = 100$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Compute the sum of the first 7 terms of } 2 - 6 + 18 - 54 + \cdots
hints: Common ratio is $r = -3$ (negative!). $S_n = a_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$.
questions.explanation: $a_1 = 2$, $r = -3$, $n = 7$. $S_7 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1 - (-3)^7}{1 - (-3)} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2187}{4} = \dfrac{4376}{4} = 1094$. (Sign trap: $(-3)^7$ stays negative because the exponent is odd.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A king places } 1 \text{ grain on the 1st chessboard square, } 2 \text{ on the 2nd, } 4 \text{ on the 3rd}, \\ \text{doubling each time. How many grains are on the } 64\text{th square alone?}
questions.explanation: $a_{64} = 1 \cdot 2^{63} = 9{,}223{,}372{,}036{,}854{,}775{,}808$. (This is the legendary chessboard problem — and $2^{63}$ is the maximum value of a signed 64-bit integer.)
steps: $2^{63} = 9{,}223{,}372{,}036{,}854{,}775{,}808$ — about 9.2 quintillion grains. The whole board would have $2^{64} - 1$ grains, more than humans have ever produced.

questions.promptTex: \$1000 \text{ is invested at } 6\% \text{ compounded annually.} \\ \text{After how many full years does the balance first exceed } \$2000?
steps: Round up to next integer: $n = 12$ years.

questions.promptTex: \text{A drug has a half-life of } 4 \text{ hours.} \\ \text{If a patient takes a } 200 \text{ mg dose, how much remains after } 14 \text{ hours? (Round to 1 decimal.)}
steps: Time elapsed $= 14$ hr; half-life $= 4$ hr; so $14/4 = 3.5$ half-lives.

questions.promptTex: \text{A loan is repaid by } 10 \text{ equal annual payments of } \$1000 \text{ at year-end,} \\ \text{with annual interest } 5\%. \text{ What is the loan amount (present value), to the nearest dollar?}
hints: Sum of present values is a finite GP: $\text{PV} = \sum_{k=1}^{10} \dfrac{1000}{1.05^k}$.
questions.explanation: Each payment of $\$1000$ at year $k$ is worth $\$1000 / 1.05^k$ today. Summing the GP: $\text{PV} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{1 - 1.05^{-10}}{0.05} = 1000 \cdot 7.7217 \approx \$7722$.
steps: Each future payment discounted: payment at year $k$ is worth $1000 / 1.05^k$ today.
Sum is a finite GP with first term $1000/1.05$ and ratio $1/1.05$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A piece of paper } 0.1 \text{ mm thick is folded in half } 20 \text{ times.} \\ \text{How tall is the resulting stack, in meters? (Round to 1 decimal.)}
hints: Each fold doubles the thickness: thickness $= 0.1 \cdot 2^{20}$ mm.
$2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$. Convert mm to m at the end.
questions.explanation: Thickness after $20$ folds = $0.1 \cdot 2^{20} = 0.1 \cdot 1{,}048{,}576 = 104{,}857.6$ mm $= 104.9$ m. (Surprising: 20 folds turns a sheet into a 30-story building.)
steps: Each fold doubles thickness — geometric growth.

questions.promptTex: \text{Inside a square of side 1, draw a smaller square whose vertices are the midpoints of the sides.} \\ \text{Inside that, do the same. Repeat forever.} \\ \text{Sum of areas of ALL the squares (including the first)?}
hints: Each new square has area exactly half of the previous (use the Pythagorean theorem on the midpoint triangle).
Then it's an infinite GP with $a_1 = 1$, $r = 1/2$. Sum $= a_1/(1 - r)$.
questions.explanation: Each midpoint-square has half the area of its parent. So areas form $1, 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, \ldots$ — infinite GP with $r = 1/2$. Sum $= \dfrac{1}{1 - 1/2} = 2$.
steps: Side of inner square: midpoint-to-midpoint distance is $\sqrt{(1/2)^2 + (1/2)^2} = \sqrt{1/2}$.
So inner area = $1/2$ of outer area.

questions.promptTex: \text{Evaluate the geometric series: } 1 + 3 + 9 + 27 + \cdots, \quad n = 8.
\text{Evaluate the geometric series: } 3 + 9 + 27 + 81 + \cdots, \quad n = 9.

hints: $S_n = a_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$. Plug in and solve for $r^n$.
$(-2)^n = -128$. Which power of $-2$ gives $-128$? Note odd powers give negative.
Reduce to $5^n = ?$ — recognize as a power of $5$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find } n: \quad \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} -4 \cdot 6^{i-1} = -223948.
\text{Find } n: \quad \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} 6^{k-1} = 9331.
\text{Evaluate: } \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} 2^{i-1}
questions.explanation: $r = 2$. Infinite GP converges iff $|r| < 1$. Here $|r| = 2 \geq 1$, so the series diverges (terms $1, 2, 4, 8, \ldots$ grow without bound).
steps: $|r| = 2 \geq 1$, so series diverges — no finite sum exists.

questions.promptTex: \text{Evaluate: } \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} 2 \cdot (-4)^{i-1}
questions.explanation: $r = -4$, $|r| = 4 \geq 1$. Diverges. (Terms $2, -8, 32, -128, \ldots$ alternate but grow in magnitude.)
steps: Series diverges — alternating sign doesn't help when terms grow.

questions.promptTex: \text{Evaluate the infinite GP with } a_1 = -\dfrac{2}{3}, \; r = -\dfrac{3}{4}.
\text{Evaluate: } -\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{8} + \dfrac{1}{16} - \cdots
\text{An infinite geometric series has } a_1 = 64 \text{ and sum } S = \dfrac{256}{3}. \\ \text{Find the common ratio } r.
\text{An infinite GP has } a_1 = \dfrac{1}{3} \text{ and sum } S = \dfrac{2}{3}. \\ \text{Find } r.
